title: Understanding the Varroa lifecycle
subtitle: What the mite does in the hive, and why timing treatment matters.
excerpt: Varroa destructor reproduces inside sealed brood cells. Most of a mite population is hidden in capped cells at any point in the season, which is why counting the mites falling through the floor gives only a partial picture. Knowing where the mite spends its time makes treatment timing make sense.
sourceNotes: National Bee Unit Beekeeper's Toolkit, nationalbeeunit.com. BBKA varroa management guidance. Rosenkranz, P., Aumeier, P., Ziegelmann, B. (2010), Biology and control of Varroa destructor, Journal of Invertebrate Pathology 103.

glossaryTerms.definition: Varroa destructor, a parasitic mite native to Asian honeybees that has spread globally. It feeds on bee fat body tissue in the brood phase and on adult bees in the phoretic phase.
A Varroa mite that is riding on an adult worker bee between reproductive cycles. Phoretic mites are the phase accessible to oxalic acid treatments.
The sequence in which a foundress mite enters a brood cell just before capping, lays eggs on the larva, and produces male and female offspring that mate inside the cell before the adult bee emerges.

content.text: Varroa
is a mite that completes most of its life inside a sealed brood cell. Understanding that one fact changes the way you read a daily mite drop and the way you schedule treatment.
The phoretic phase
Between reproductive cycles, a Varroa mite rides on the body of an adult bee. This is the
phoretic

phase, and it is the only point at which the mite is exposed to treatments that don't penetrate capped cells. Oxalic acid vaporisation and trickle treatment work on phoretic mites. Sealed brood protects the mites inside it entirely.
In a colony with active brood, only around 10 to 15 percent of mites are phoretic at any one time. The majority are completing a
reproduction cycle
inside worker or drone cells.
The brood phase

The foundress mite enters a brood cell when the larva is about five days old, just before capping. Once inside the sealed cell, she lays her first egg (male) after around seventy hours, followed by female eggs at thirty-hour intervals. The male and the first female to mature will mate inside the cell.

A foundress in a worker cell typically produces one mated female offspring per cycle. In a drone cell, which is sealed for longer (twenty-four days versus twelve for workers), she can produce two or three. This is why colonies with heavy drone brood in spring carry higher mite loads by summer.

Not every foundress successfully reproduces. First-time foundresses in worker cells often fail to time their egg-laying correctly. Reproductive success varies by season and colony. The important point is that the mite population grows exponentially with the brood cycle, not linearly.
What the mite drop tells you

The natural mite drop onto the open mesh floor is the count of mites dying from natural causes or being groomed off adult bees. A count of more than ten mites per day in summer suggests a high infestation. But the drop counts only phoretic mites and those dying in cells, the reproductive mites are not counted.
The
varroa mite count

using an alcohol wash or sugar roll gives a more reliable proportion of phoretic mites in the adult bee sample. The alcohol wash is more accurate; both are much better than the floor drop for estimating total infestation.
Why treatment timing matters

The most effective point to treat is when the colony is broodless or near-broodless. In winter (late November to early January in the UK), a colony with no capped brood has almost all its mites in the phoretic phase. An oxalic acid treatment at this point hits the entire population.

Summer treatments (thymol, formic acid) work on phoretic mites and have some penetration into capped cells. They are necessary to hold the population down through the season, but they will not achieve the same knockdown as a winter broodless treatment.

Treating in autumn before the winter bees are reared is the other critical window. Mites that parasitise the winter bees shorten those bees' lives and weaken the cluster. A colony that goes into winter carrying a high mite load on its winter bees often does not make it to March.
